Let's start by orienting to where we are in the TDB process. By now, you should have a reasonably accurate description of a minimum viable audience you seek to serve, initially, an in-depth understanding of that audience's needs and desires expressed in their own words. At least one expression of a value creating idea to share with that audience for initial feedback. Minimum viable infrastructure to support hypothesis testing. A marketing hypothesis to test. If you don't have all of these ingredients yet, that's okay. However, this module requires everything in the list above before you can move forward effectively. We're going to use Facebook's advertising platform to generate highly targeted awareness for our ideas. That awareness provides the fuel to test our experiments and the data to validate and improve upon our ideas before we've committed significant time, energy, and dollars. There are other ways to generate traffic, but we believe that Facebook is the best option for validating ideas for several reasons. First, we can target audiences narrowly using demographics, for example, age, gender, geography, and psychographics, for example, interests. The more clearly we know who we're advertising to the more meaningful the results will be. Second, Facebook's advertising platform allows long copy ads, 700 to 900 words will be our approximate target. We can make our case in our ads effectively, which attracts the right prospects and repels the wrong prospects. Third, Facebook provides data that creates more texture in our results. For example, we'll know who clicks on our ads to read more versus who clicks on our ads to go to a landing page. We'll also see when our ads are shared, which is a strong social signal indicating value and various audience reactions. For example, likes and other emotional responses. And, perhaps most importantly, we'll be able to read and respond to comments. Fourth, Facebook allows us to frame our ideas in the minds of our audience and that can have profound downstream effects. More on framing later. It's important to mention that TDB is not a how-to-facebook advertising course. We're not going to tell you how to set up advertising accounts and how to push the right buttons in the right order. That's well beyond the scope of this course. However, we will share several free resources in this module that will help and we encourage you to review all of them. Start by reading our 10-part mini-course How to Build a Happy Customer Creating Engine with paid traffic. That's a 30,000 view of paid traffic that explores three questions. What is paid traffic? What is a traffic engine? Why should you care? Next, review the get started with Facebook and get started with advertising content on Facebook's blueprint training. Pay particular attention to add policies for content, creative, and targeting. Under Learn Advanced Buying Options, watch Create Facebook Ads. Then watch all of the content in the section titled Target the Right Audience. When you're ready to set up your advertising account, review Facebook's Ads Manager. We realize this seems like a lot of additional work. However, it's worth it and the time you invest now is time well spent. If you are a customer of the traffic engine, reread module 6.5, Facebook Offer and Messaging Testing Framework. That has direct application to TDB. In future TDB modules, we'll share screencasts of our work for Project X, including Facebook Setup, Hypothesis Testing, and Review. Before we dive into specifics, let's identify some quicksand we want you to avoid. First, avoid language in your ads that calls out people directly. Conventional wisdom for advertising copies to identify the prospect's pain and then amplify it. Don't do that on Facebook. For example, if you owe the IRS more than $10,000 in back taxes might get attention in a banner ad, but it's not the type of messaging Facebook wants on its platform. Second, avoid claims with time frames, even if they're true. For example, lose 10 pounds in 10 days is a claim, lose 10 pounds, and a time frame in 10 days. There's a lot of nuance. Of course, you might be telling your own story, for example, and you might have lost 10 pounds in 10 days. However, claims with time frames seem to have a near magical ability to attract negative attention from Facebook and we recommend avoiding those completely. Third, write conversationally. Facebook is a social environment and anything that feels like selling is very noticeable. Don't try to write copy or even worse, write ad copy. Instead, imagine you're speaking to an ideal prospect and write the way you would speak pro tip, read your ad copy out loud and imagine you're speaking to an ideal prospect. That's a quick way to dramatically improve your writing. In addition to avoiding problems, we also want to stack the deck in our favor. To do that, we're going to frame our ideas to increase their power, create compelling hooks to capture our audience's attention immediately. Use the rule of three to ensure our ads help prospects understand our ideas. Let's start with framing. A frame defines what's possible in your world, and what isn't, what matters, and what doesn't. The frame pulls your ideal prospects forward and repels others, ideally before they even enter your world. For example, a typical direct response style headline might include the phrase a little known secret. That's a frame that gives context for the information that follows. In rich shern's words, secrets are a form of social currency. We pay attention when we're told something is a little known secret, that's why that headline format is so popular. And wildly overused, in our opinion. The first sentence of the traffic engine manifesto is what you're about to read was 18 months and 21 years in the making. That's also a frame. The subtext of that sentence communicates a lot, including a lot of thought went into the creation of the course. It's based on decades of expertise. The person who created the course is knowledgeable. None of that was stated explicitly, it's implied by the frame. Once you become aware of frames and the power of framing, you'll notice frames everywhere. For our purposes, we're setting a frame in our Facebook ads that is in service to the idea we're validating. For example, we could frame project X as a way to generate ideas on demand, or we could create a more narrow, tactical frame for a specific audience, generate an endless stream of content ideas for your blog. How we frame our ideas affects who is interested in those ideas and how those ideas are perceived. Think about a frame as the door someone steps through to get into your world. If you're an expert in a particular field, then you can frame what you're sharing as hard earned experience, expertise. Or, if you created something to scratch your own itch, you can frame your idea as what you discovered along the way, how you can save them time because you've done the work already, something counterintuitive you're discovered, etc. For additional texture, here's a lightly edited, excerpt about frames from our messaging and writing framework. Frames are written to capture attention, generate interest for the right audience, and discourage the wrong audience. What's in it for them is the underlying structure. A frame captures the promises we're making to the reader. A really good frame says, here's something interesting, here's why it's interesting, and if you want to learn more, here's what to do next. Here are a few questions we use when creating a frame. What is my audience's core desire? If they could wave a magic wand and get exactly what they want, what would it be? It's worth highlighting that part of our job is to read between the lines because people often can't externalize their deeply felt, visceral emotional needs. What can my audience accomplish better, faster, with less effort and or lower cost as a result of what I'm going to share? What is something I know about this topic that my audience doesn't that would be interesting and valuable to them? What is one thing that, if my audience internalized it, would completely change their perspective? Think, mind blown. In what ways is conventional wisdom about this topic wrong or less effective? If someone is interested in the value I have to offer, what should I slash he do next? Use these questions as prompts. The most significant shift that informs how we write and how we think about the work we do in general is that we're continually reorienting to the perspective of the reader. What does she want most? How can we create the most value for him? Next up, Facebook ad hooks. Here's an excerpt from the traffic engine. The hook is the first two lines of a Facebook ad. That's my term. I've heard that called by many names. My two favorite hooks are dialogue and questions, which can be combined to You are never going to believe what my accountant told me about the payroll protection loan. I'm sorry, Sean, I talked to your attorney and there's nothing we can do to help. What if everything I believed about gaining muscle after turning 40 was wrong? Three things I wish every digital marketer knew about paid advertising on Google and Facebook. The opportunities are endless. The first two lines of an ad pre-qualify, so be careful about who and how you attract. At the same time, remember that the first two lines sell the rest of the ad. A little conversational curiosity to get a prospect's attention is okay, in my opinion, if the ad copy is transparent. The rule of three was first mentioned during a momentum building workshop, lean edition, landing page review. Sean realized during that conversation he hadn't ever mentioned it to anyone, including Andre. The rule of three can be used for everything you'll write in this course. It works at the micro level, for example, what to include in a single Facebook ad, and at the macro level, for example, an idea you're validating that includes a Facebook ad, landing page, and email series. Here's an overview, lightly edited to be more specifically relevant for TDB. Enter Sean. BTW, I'm sure I did not invent this idea, I just gave the framework a name when I noticed how often I use it. Structure is important to me when I write. I want to know where I'm going and the order in which I'll get there. I also want to know that my ideas flow from one to the next, that I've supported those ideas well, and that there's a natural progression from A to B to C. The rule of three starts with a controlling idea, thesis statement, this is the main point I'm making, for TDB, this is the idea you're validating. Then, I make three main points that are each accompanied by three supporting ideas. Finally, I want to direct the reader or viewer to what's next. Structurally, I imagine it like this. Controlling idea, thesis statement. Main point number one. Supporting idea number one. Supporting idea number two. Supporting idea number three. Main point number two. Supporting idea number one. Supporting idea number two. Supporting idea number three. Main point number three. Supporting idea number one. Supporting idea number two. Supporting idea number three. Call to action. This framework works for writing and editing. For example, if you're writing a 700-900-word Facebook ad, start with the main takeaway you want the reader to believe, that's your controlling idea, whatever you're trying to validate. Then, identify the three things that person needs to believe in order to accept your controlling idea. Make sure those ideas make sense and that they follow logically from one to the other. They're not just random ideas thrown on the page. Next, support each of those three main points with, up to, three proof points. Finally, after you've made your case, like a prosecutor, tell the reader what to do next. If you're editing your work or another person's work, map their ideas to the rule of three structure. This can be an eye-opening exercise. Is the controlling idea obvious? Do the main points flow? Are they supported with evidence? Does the CTA lead naturally to what's next? I suspect some of you are wondering does it have to be three main points and three supporting ideas? No. These are guidelines. However, three by three works very well in most circumstances. Judgment and discretion are required. And yes, for those of you who are a student observers, the rule of three is fractal. There are three parts, controlling idea, making the case, call to action, and within each part are three additional parts. If you're writing much longer copy, you might include three sub points under each supporting idea, for example. It's threes within threes within threes. Our brains seem to understand groups of three well. The key to this framework's effectiveness is its simplicity. Don't overthink it. Okay, let's translate all of this into action with the following homework assignment. Read this module once, beginning to end, to get a general sense of what we're discussing. Reread this module a second time, and take notes. Identify any questions you have, ask those in the comments below. Determine where you want to start, Facebook account setup or writing your first ad. If you want to set up your Facebook account first, review the free paid traffic mini course and Facebook blueprint training above. Take notes and, when you're ready, set up your Facebook advertising account. When you're ready to create your first Facebook ad, identify your controlling idea first. What is the main takeaway someone needs to believe in order to take a step closer toward your idea, by opting in, scheduling a call, etc. Spend however much time it takes to get this right because everything that follows is affected by your controlling idea. Clearly identify the frame you're using to present your perspective to your audience. Follow the rule of three to write a 700 to 900 word conversational Facebook ad. Imagine you're speaking to an ideal prospect. Make sure your ad has a compelling hook that attracts attention. Take here to open a high-res image in a new window. We encourage you to share your ad copy in the TDB community for feedback. Yes, we know that's scary, do it anyway. Next up we'll explain how to track performance, review data, and confirm, or disconfirm, a marketing hypothesis. That'll be Validate, Part 5. Ask questions in the comments below and discuss in the community. Andrei and Sean.
